judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all three expectations with essentially equivalent substance and correct reasoning; Report 2 is somewhat more thorough (checks for RLS/migrations, notes independent verification) but this doesn't change the expectation coverage — the two are comparable in quality on the graded criteria.

report_1.evidence: search_appointments(session, tenant_id, q, status) receives tenant_id as an argument but never uses it. The raw SQL it builds ... has no tenant_id predicate at all. ... any authenticated user of any tenant can search and read every other tenant's appointment notes field (clinical notes), plus their tenant_id, provider, and appointment times

report_2.evidence: There is no tenant_id clause. The tenant_id argument is accepted by the function signature (search_appointments(session, tenant_id, q, status)) but never referenced in the query body. ... any authenticated user from Tenant A can search and read Tenant B's (and every other tenant's) appointment records, including clinical notes, provider names, and timestamps

report_1.evidence: The PR changes build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id) to build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters) with filters as a required positional argument (no default). backend/app/exports.py::nightly_export — which this PR does not touch — still calls build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id).all() with only two arguments. This will raise TypeError... i.e. the nightly per-tenant CSV export job breaks for every tenant

report_2.evidence: On feat/appointment-search, git show feat/appointment-search:backend/app/db.py shows the signature changed to build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters), where filters is a required third positional parameter with no default ... backend/app/exports.py is untouched by this PR ... and still calls build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id) with two arguments at line 9, inside nightly_export. This will raise TypeError...

report_1.evidence: Because the endpoint parameter is typed status: AppointmentStatus (a str Enum with only booked/cancelled/completed), FastAPI/Pydantic validates the value before this function ever sees it, so I don't believe this is currently exploitable for SQL injection. ... I'm not blocking on it since I can't construct an actual injection given the enum constraint.

report_2.evidence: AppointmentStatus is a closed str, Enum (booked/cancelled/completed), and FastAPI/Pydantic rejects any request where the status query parameter isn't one of those literal values before the handler runs — so this is not exploitable today. ... it does not need to block this merge
